findings.ruleDescription: Detects patterns where external input is interpolated into shell commands. Enables arbitrary command execution if input is not sanitized.
findings.recommendation: This code passes unsanitized input to shell commands. An attacker could inject additional commands (e.g., "; rm -rf /") to execute arbitrary operations. Use parameterized commands or input validation.

findings.ruleDescription: Detects patterns where remote content is piped directly to a shell interpreter. This allows arbitrary code execution from a remote server. Frequently seen in malicious skills that download and execute payloads.
findings.reportTitle: Remote code download and execution detected
findings.recommendation: This code downloads content from the internet and executes it directly. An attacker can change the remote payload at any time. This is one of the most dangerous patterns in agent skills. Do not install.

fullDescription.text: Detects patterns where remote content is piped directly to a shell interpreter. This allows arbitrary code execution from a remote server. Frequently seen in malicious skills that download and execute payloads.

message.text: Remote code download and execution detected

findings.ruleDescription: Detects outbound HTTP/HTTPS requests to domains that don't match the tool's declared purpose. MCP servers making unexpected network calls may be exfiltrating data or phoning home to C2 infrastructure.
findings.recommendation: Review all network calls. Verify each URL belongs to the service the tool is supposed to interact with. Unexpected POST requests are the highest-risk pattern.

findings.ruleTitle: Dynamic code execution via eval or Function constructor
findings.ruleDescription: Detects usage of eval(), new Function(), or similar dynamic code execution patterns that allow arbitrary code to run. In MCP servers and tool code, these are rarely legitimate and often indicate payload delivery mechanisms.
findings.recommendation: Review the context of eval/Function usage carefully. In MCP servers, legitimate uses are extremely rare. This likely indicates a code injection vector.

findings.ruleDescription: Detects patterns that could enable path traversal attacks, allowing access to files outside intended directories. Common in MCP servers that handle file operations without proper path sanitization.
findings.recommendation: This code accesses file system paths using external input without apparent sanitization. An attacker could use path traversal (../) to read or write files outside the intended directory. Review file access patterns carefully.

findings.ruleDescription: Detects skills and MCP servers that make overbroad capability claims, stuff keywords for discovery abuse, or impersonate trusted vendors. Used to trick agents and users into choosing malicious tools over legitimate ones.
findings.reportTitle: Capability inflation or vendor impersonation detected
findings.recommendation: This tool makes suspicious claims — impersonating trusted vendors, claiming to do "everything", or using keyword stuffing. Legitimate tools have specific, honest descriptions. This may be a social engineering attempt to get you to install a malicious tool.

matches.description: Claims that the tool can do anything or everything

fullDescription.text: Detects skills and MCP servers that make overbroad capability claims, stuff keywords for discovery abuse, or impersonate trusted vendors. Used to trick agents and users into choosing malicious tools over legitimate ones.

message.text: Capability inflation or vendor impersonation detected

matchDetails.description: Claims that the tool can do anything or everything

properties.recommendation: This tool makes suspicious claims — impersonating trusted vendors, claiming to do "everything", or using keyword stuffing. Legitimate tools have specific, honest descriptions. This may be a social engineering attempt to get you to install a malicious tool.

findings.ruleTitle: File size anomaly for file type
findings.ruleDescription: Flags files whose size significantly exceeds the expected range for their file type. A tailwind.config.js at 7KB when typical is 1-2KB, or a SKILL.md at 50KB when typical is 2-5KB, suggests hidden content.
findings.reportTitle: File size or structure anomaly detected
findings.recommendation: Inspect the file for hidden content, especially at the end of long lines or after the apparent end of meaningful code.

matches.description: Config file more than 8x expected size for its type
